name: Equivalent Fractions — Different Names, Same Amount
whatIsIt: Equivalent fractions are different fraction names for the SAME amount. 1/2, 2/4, and 4/8 all cover exactly the same space — like one person being called 'Amma' at home and 'Doctor' at work. Same person, different names!
whyNeeded: To add, subtract, or compare fractions, we often need pieces of the same size. Equivalent fractions let us re-cut a fraction into smaller equal pieces WITHOUT changing the amount — that is the magic key that unlocks fraction addition and comparison later.
realLifeUses: Sharing: half a chapati is the same amount as 2 quarter-pieces — useful when more friends arrive
Money: 1/2 rupee = 50 paise = 2 coins of 25 paise (2/4 of a rupee)
Time: half an hour = 30/60 minutes = 2/4 of an hour — the clock says all three the same way
Cooking: a 1/2 cup measure can be filled with two 1/4 cups when the big cup is missing

vocabulary.meaning: Equal in value — same amount, even if it looks different.
Fractions that name the same amount, like 1/2 and 2/4.
Rename a fraction with the smallest numbers possible: 6/8 becomes 3/4.

story.title: Birbal Settles the Buttermilk Quarrel
story.text: On a scorching day two farmers came to Emperor Akbar's court, quarrelling. 'I drank half a glass of buttermilk!' said one. 'And I drank two-fourths of the same-size glass — so I drank MORE!' said the other. Akbar turned to clever Birbal. Birbal poured both amounts into matching glasses and held them side by side. 'Look — the buttermilk reaches exactly the same line. One-half and two-fourths are just two NAMES for the very same amount. Neither of you drank more.' The farmers laughed and shared the rest.
story.extractedProblem: One farmer drank 1/2 of a glass and another drank 2/4 of an identical glass. Did one of them drink more?
story.answerInStory: No — 1/2 and 2/4 are equivalent fractions. They drank exactly the same amount.

visual.caption: Three names, one amount. The shaded parts end at exactly the same place — 1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8.

teachingGallery.title: Equivalent fractions — same amount, new name
teachingGallery.note: Multiply top AND bottom by the same number: the picture doesn't change.

standardMethod.summary: To make an equivalent fraction, multiply (or divide) the TOP and the BOTTOM by the SAME number.
steps: Multiply the top by it AND the bottom by it — both, always both!
To simplify, divide both by the same number instead: 6/8 → (6÷2)/(8÷2) = 3/4.

alternateMethods.name: Folding method (paper proof)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE why two fractions are equal, not just calculate.
steps: Fold a strip in half and shade 1/2.
Fold the SAME strip in half again — now it shows 4 parts, and 2 are shaded.
Every extra fold doubles both numbers but keeps the amount the same.
alternateMethods.example: One strip, three folds: the same shading reads 1/2, then 2/4, then 4/8.

alternateMethods.name: Number line check
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To verify two fractions are equivalent.
steps: Draw two number lines from 0 to 1, one above the other.
Cut one into 2 jumps, the other into 4 jumps.
Mark 1/2 on the first and 2/4 on the second.
If the marks sit at the SAME spot, the fractions are equivalent.
alternateMethods.example: 1/2 and 2/4 land on exactly the same point of the line — equivalent!

steps: The bottom went from 2 to 6. What did we multiply by? 2 × 3 = 6, so by 3.
Do the SAME to the top: 1 × 3 = 3.

visual.caption: Line them up: 1/2 and 3/6 stretch to the same length — same amount, different name.

workedExamples.problem: Ravi says 2/3 = 3/4 because 'I added 1 to the top and 1 to the bottom.' Check with strips: is he right?
steps: Fold one strip into 3 parts, shade 2: that is 2/3.
Fold another equal strip into 4 parts, shade 3: that is 3/4.
Hold them together: the shaded ends do NOT line up — 3/4 is a little more.
ADDING the same number to top and bottom changes the amount. Only MULTIPLYING or DIVIDING both keeps it equal.
workedExamples.answer: No — adding to top and bottom does not make equivalent fractions.

trickPractice.trick: Multiply top and bottom the same
trickPractice.intro: Whatever you do to the bottom, do to the top — the fraction's value stays put.

questions.q: 1/2 = ?/4  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 2 → 4? Multiply by 2.
Do the SAME to the top: 1 × 2.
Missing top = 2.

questions.q: 1/2 = ?/6  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 2 → 6? Multiply by 3.
Do the SAME to the top: 1 × 3.
Missing top = 3.

questions.q: 1/3 = ?/6  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 3 → 6? Multiply by 2.
Do the SAME to the top: 1 × 2.
Missing top = 2.

questions.q: 3/4 = ?/8  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 4 → 8? Multiply by 2.
Do the SAME to the top: 3 × 2.
Missing top = 6.

questions.q: 2/5 = ?/10  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 5 → 10? Multiply by 2.
Do the SAME to the top: 2 × 2.
Missing top = 4.

questions.q: 2/3 = ?/9  — what is the missing TOP number?
hintLadder: How do you get 3 → 9? Multiply by 3.
Do the SAME to the top: 2 × 3.
Missing top = 6.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the same number to top and bottom (2/3 → 3/4) and thinking the amount stayed the same.
commonMistakes.fix: Equivalents come from MULTIPLYING or DIVIDING both numbers by the same number — never adding. Check with strips: do the shaded ends line up?

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying only the top (or only the bottom): 1/2 → 2/2.
commonMistakes.fix: Both or nothing! Whatever you do to the top, do exactly the same to the bottom. One-sided changes change the amount.

commonMistakes.mistake: Flipping the fraction while renaming it (1/2 becoming 4/2 instead of 2/4).
commonMistakes.fix: Keep the fraction the same way up. Denominator stays Down — multiply it in its place.

hintLadder: Multiply the top AND the bottom of 1/2 by the same number.
Try multiplying both by 2: what do you get?
1×2 = 2 on top, 2×2 = 4 below.
easy.explain: 1/2 = 2/4 — both numbers multiplied by 2, amount unchanged.

easy.q: Fill in the missing top number: 1/2 = ?/4 (write the whole fraction, like 2/4)
hintLadder: The bottom went from 2 to 4. What was it multiplied by?
By 2! Now do the same to the top: 1 × 2 = ?
Top becomes 2, bottom is 4 — write the full fraction.
Bottom: 3 became 6. Multiplied by what?
By 2. Do the same to the top: 2 × 2 = ?
Top 4, bottom 6.

medium.q: Anu wrote 3/4 = 4/5. What mistake did she make?

options.label: She added 1 to the top and bottom instead of multiplying

medium.answer: She added 1 to the top and bottom instead of multiplying
hintLadder: Compare 3→4 and 4→5. Was that multiplying or adding?
Does adding the same number to both keep the amount equal? Check with strips.
3/4 and 4/5 sit at different spots on the number line — she added instead of multiplied.

challenge.q: Simplify 6/8 to its smallest name (write the fraction).
hintLadder: Simplifying means DIVIDING top and bottom by the same number.
What number divides both 6 and 8 exactly?
Divide both by 2: 6÷2 on top, 8÷2 below.

challenge.q: A recipe needs 1/2 cup of milk, but you only have a 1/8 cup measure. How many 1/8 cups make 1/2?
hintLadder: Rename 1/2 with 8 at the bottom: 1/2 = ?/8.
Bottom 2→8 means multiply by 4. Do the same on top.
1/2 = 4/8, so four 1/8 cups fill it.

options.label: They name the same amount, like 1/2 and 2/4

questions.answer: They name the same amount, like 1/2 and 2/4
hintLadder: Think of the juice story — same amount, different names.
What did the folded strips show?
Equivalent = equal value, even with different numbers.
Bottom 3 → 6: multiplied by what?
By 2. Same to the top.
1 × 2 = 2 on top, 6 below.
Divide top and bottom by the same number.
What divides both 4 and 8? Try 4.

questions.q: To make a fraction equivalent, you must…

options.label: Multiply or divide the top AND bottom by the same number
Add the same number to the top and bottom

questions.answer: Multiply or divide the top AND bottom by the same number
hintLadder: Both or nothing — which operation keeps the amount equal?
Adding shifts the amount; check 2/3 vs 3/4 with strips.
Only multiplying/dividing BOTH keeps the strips lined up.

teachBackPrompt: Ravi still thinks 2/4 is bigger than 1/2 'because 2 is more than 1.' Use the folding story (or your own drawing) to teach Ravi why 1/2 and 2/4 are the same amount, and tell him the one rule for making equivalent fractions.

revisionCard.summary: Equivalent fractions = same amount, different names (1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8). Rule: multiply OR divide top and bottom by the SAME number — never add. Simplify = divide both to the smallest name (6/8 → 3/4).

gameMission.title: The Name-Tag Party
gameMission.brief: Every fraction at the party wears many name-tags! Match the guests who are secretly the same amount (1/2 with 2/4 and 4/8) before Ravi mixes up the tags with his 'just add 1' trick.

realLifeProject: In the kitchen with a parent: fill a 1/2 cup with water. Now pour it into 1/4 cups — how many did it fill? Write the equivalence you discovered (1/2 = 2/4) in your math notebook and decorate it with name-tags.

rememberIt.hook: Equivalent = same amount, different numbers. Times/divide top AND bottom by the same
rememberIt.unpack: 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 50/100. Do the same thing to top and bottom and the value does not change.

studentQuestions.a: Same slice of pizza cut differently. Multiply top and bottom of 1/2 by 2 -> 2/4. Same amount.

studentQuestions.q: How do I make an equivalent fraction?
studentQuestions.a: Multiply (or divide) BOTH top and bottom by the same number. 2/3 times 3 -> 6/9.
